summary: Zed Shaw announces his move from San Francisco to Miami, reflecting on his seven years in the Bay Area with mixed feelings. He credits San Francisco for launching his book career, learning to paint, and memorable moments at Union Square and Fort Mason, but criticizes the city's toxic culture war between tech bros and anti-tech libertines. He chose Miami for its lack of state income tax (important for his plan to study art overseas), its art scene, and its generally happier vibe. The post is structured as a farewell love letter to San Francisco that doubles as an indictment of the city's personality.
key_points: San Francisco's culture is ruined by the constant war between brogrammers riding tech wealth and anti-tech libertines, making it an ugly place to live and work
Shaw's time in SF was personally transformative — he built a writing career, learned to paint, and healed his guitar-playing hands at Union Square
California's aggressive overseas tax collection policies were a practical factor in leaving the state
Shaw personifies cities as lovers and personalities: NYC as a bipolar court jester, SF as a depressed brilliant failed artist, Miami as someone too fabulous to care
Miami was chosen for art development, lower cost, better weather, and proximity to overseas relocation plans
money_quotes: As the money in tech prolapsed into the local economy the brogrammers in the industry confused their acquisition of a loan with being real super dudes.
This constant fight between the wannabe libertines just trying to get whipped at The Citadel, and the brogrammers living off Zuck's scraps, ends up ruining what could be a fantastic place to live.
San Francisco will always be that lover who made me feel awesome and successful while cheating on me and calling me a 'fag nerd'.

The personality of New York was a bipolar court jester. There were days you could tell NYC was pissed off at you, other days where Gotham loved you like a mother, and most of the time it was fucking hilarious.
San Francisco's personality is definitely that of a depressed but brilliant failed artist.
When you live in a city you can sort of feel a personality permeating everything.
I don't know what Miami is like yet, but I'm getting the sense that Miami actually doesn't give a fuck what I think about her. Miami is looking too fabulous to care.
key_insight: Cities shape the people who live in them, and sometimes the healthiest thing is to leave before you fully absorb a city's dysfunction.
